text:
SPRINGFIELD, Mass. (WGGB/WSHM) - Safe swimming was a focus for some of Springfield’s youngest residents early Saturday at Central High School.

Splashing with safety, the Lambda Beta Sigma Alumnae Chapter of Sigma Gamma Rho, one of the largest black sororities in the nation stepped up to help give men, women, boys, girls the courage to step off the diving board and into the water.

“Yes, it’s about learning to swim, it’s also about finding the next Olympic swimmer,” said Maritza McClendon.

Well, they’ll be standing right next to Maritza McClendon, not only was she the guest instructor at the free national event co-sponsored by the U.S. swimming, she’s a decorated Olympian and a history maker.

She’s the first black woman to make it on the nation’s Olympic swim team took home sliver in Athens back in 2004.

For her, it’s not just a profession, it’s personal.

“If you don’t know, 64 percent of African-American children don’t know how to swim; and 45 percent of Hispanic children don’t know how to swim,” explained McClendon. “So, it’s really important to introduce them to the education as to why we do water safety.”

It’s a lesson, Yocelyn Melendez wants for her kids.

“I don’t know how to swim and it’s affected my life negatively so I really want them to learn how to swim,” expressed Melendez. “There’s a lot of open lakes and rivers that we like to go to, but they can’t swim, so thankfully with this they will be able to learn at least the basics of how to swim or how to save themselves.”

It goes beyond the teaching, there’s also a push for these young ones to dream.

“I was that little girl, who didn’t know how to swim, watched the Olympics and watched them get their medal put on them and hear the star-spangled-banner and I was immediately inspired so that what we’re trying to do,” added McClendon.

title: Olympic medalist spreads awareness on water safety in Springfield
description: Safe swimming was a focus for some of Springfield’s youngest residents early Saturday at Central High School.
